steps.text: Initiate an intralaboratory method validation study.
Repeat some or all of the performance tests.
Initiate an interlaboratory method validation study.
Repeat some or all aspects of the interlaboratory study.
Clearly and completely state the measurement process components validated and the combination of procedures composing the method.
Clearly define the purpose of the method (measurement objectives) and the intended use of the data.
Define and clearly describe the method scope and applicability, including validated components, analytes/matrices, analyte levels, limitations/assumptions, and data quality objectives.
Evaluate the method performance characteristics, including selectivity, instrument calibration, bias/trueness, precision, quantitation limits and range, and detection limits.
Evaluate selectivity by testing appropriate matrix blanks and fortified (spiked) matrix blanks.
Test a worst-case sample matrix, as defined in the scope of a method, during both intra- and interlaboratory validation studies.
Study actual samples of material containing an undetermined amount of native analyte to evaluate method selectivity.

Use multiple analytical procedures (such as sample preparation, wet-chemistry, and/or instrumental analysis procedures) during an intralaboratory validation study to substantiate the selectivity of the method being evaluated.
Demonstrate the characteristics of a calibration function and justification for a selected calibration model during an intralaboratory method validation study.
Evaluate trueness minimally at the extremes of the quantitation range stated in the scope of a method, and at regulatory levels.
Evaluate method repeatability and reproducibility at the extremes of the quantitation range stated in the scope of the method, and at regulatory levels.
Clearly state the repeatability and reproducibility conditions to allow proper interpretation of the measures of dispersion.
Evaluate method trueness and precision at the extremes of the quantitation range.
Verify defined limits (such as those based on specifications for precision) by testing materials containing analyte at the claimed Limit of Quantitation (LOQ).
Define the term 'detection limit' and provide a description of how it was evaluated during method validation.
Verify limits derived from mathematical definitions or statistical models by testing materials containing analyte at the claimed detection level.
Perform ruggedness testing using experimental designs, such as a fractional factorial design, to examine method performance under minor variations in operating or environmental conditions.
Identify critical control points in the documented method.
Initiate and conduct the interlaboratory method validation study.
Document any deviations from the interlaboratory study protocol that occur.
Prepare a method validation report addressing the required topic areas.
Publish the EPA method for general use.
Demonstrate selectivity in the presence of species known or predicted to present analytical challenges.
Generate sufficient data to demonstrate and justify the method-recommended calibration approach.
Evaluate trueness using samples that span the degree of analytical difficulty encompassed by the specified scope and applicability.
Evaluate the method for both repeatability (within-lab) and reproducibility (among labs).
Characterize analytical measurement uncertainty over a quantitation range.
Verify detection limit estimates by analyzing samples containing analyte at the claimed detection level.
Perform ruggedness testing by making small changes in operating or environmental conditions.
Include the design and results of the interlaboratory study in the method validation summary report.
Report data from the interlaboratory study in tabular form.
Include a discussion describing the details of, and rationale for, any changes made to the method resulting from the interlaboratory study.
Ensure proper sample preservation and storage stability studies were performed during method development.
Investigate the stability of the analyte(s) from the time of sampling through the time of analysis.
Investigate the extract stability.
Peer review the chemical method of analysis (or review by a documented alternate procedure).
Publish the chemical method of analysis.

preconditions.text: Substantial evidence exists that a method is a good candidate for publication as an EPA method.
A detailed procedure has been written, operational parameters are believed to be optimized and finalized, and no additional changes are anticipated.
Procedural changes are made during the intralaboratory study that may affect the method performance characteristics.
A successful intralaboratory study has been completed.
Procedural changes are required after initiating a multiple laboratory study that may affect the method performance characteristics.
Adequate representations of matrix blanks cannot be obtained or prepared
The quantitation range must include regulatory levels.
A successful intralaboratory study must be completed.
The issuing entity must demonstrate a pressing programmatic need for publishing a national method.
An interlaboratory method validation study must be completed.
The method is designed for demonstrating compliance with regulatory requirements.
Detection limit estimates are derived from mathematical definitions or statistics.

constraints.text: A combination of procedures or techniques that are based on different measurement principles should be used.

The reference materials approach introduces the important uncertainty of matrix matching, and the reliability of a bias estimate depends upon the relationship between the composition of the reference material and the samples.
When using the alternate method approach, the reliability of a bias estimate is dependent upon how much is known about the performance characteristics of the alternate method.
The matrix spiking approach introduces uncertainty regarding the behavior of spiked materials, compared to materials containing native analyte.
A quantitation range must include either a regulatory or other type of action level.
The fractional factorial experimental design is not intended for method optimization purposes.
Deviations from the interlaboratory study protocol should be strongly discouraged.
The validation report must address background information, validation techniques, changes made, and recommendations for future work.
Selectivity is especially important at action levels of concern.
The changes in operating or environmental conditions should reflect expected, reasonable variations likely to be encountered in different laboratories.
Analytes may be lost through volatilization, sorption, chemical degradation (abiotic reactions), and microbial degradation.
Microorganisms have the potential to degrade target analytes and represent a significant pathway for the fate and destruction of organic compounds.
In emergency response situations, follow the EPA emergency response method validation policy (Directive Number FEM-2010-01).
